text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Aisha Karim
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Aisha Karim
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Murphy, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH-829476 NHS No. 9475201836  
12 Faraday Mews, London, SE15 4QZ

Thank you for asking me to review your patient. I saw him in follow-up today with his partner present. Unfortunately, there has been a clear deterioration in his seizure control over recent months. He works in a cleanroom/data-hall environment with strict access controls. He reports that retrieval of rescue medication and arranging timely refills are often significantly delayed due to site procedures, and he has consequently missed doses on several occasions. He describes more frequent events, with clusters occurring up to 7 in bad weeks, and shorter inter-seizure intervals compared with earlier this year.

He has a history consistent with combined generalised and focal epilepsy. He and his partner report two main semiologies: (1) episodes beginning with a rising epigastric sensation, brief behavioural arrest, rightward head turn and lip-smacking lasting 30–60 seconds, followed by several minutes of confusion; and (2) generalised convulsive seizures without warning, with bilateral tonic–clonic activity, tongue biting and myalgia after recovery. Nocturnal events have been noted on two occasions this month by wearable alert, and there has been one daytime convulsion at work necessitating first-aid support. Post-ictal recovery is taking longer (30–90 minutes) than previously. There are no consistent triggers other than the missed/late medication doses related to workplace access constraints.

He reports strict adherence when able to access his medication, and he carries written confirmation from Occupational Health outlining his need for rapid access to anti-seizure medicines; however, he states that on multiple shifts he was unable to leave the controlled area promptly, resulting in delayed dosing. He has reduced confidence at work and has avoided overtime due to fear of clusters. There is no current alcohol or recreational drug use, and sleep is suboptimal on rotating shifts.

Examination today was non-focal. Cardiovascular observations were normal and there were no interictal neurological deficits. There were no features to suggest an acute intercurrent illness. Review of previous investigations confirms a diagnosis of combined generalised and focal epilepsy: prior EEGs demonstrated generalised spike–wave discharges and independent left temporal sharp waves; MRI brain (2023) showed no structural lesion.

Current treatment: Levetiracetam 1.5 g twice daily; Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily; rescue Midazolam buccal 10 mg as needed for prolonged seizures. He reports that the rescue medication is stored off-floor and requires supervisor release, leading to delays. Pharmacy refills are reportedly postponed when he is on long shifts, contributing to missed evening doses.

In summary, this is a patient with combined generalised and focal epilepsy who is experiencing a worsening trajectory of seizure control, with clusters up to 7 in bad weeks, temporally associated with delayed access to regular and rescue medication within a high-security workplace. While his baseline regimen has previously been effective, the emerging pattern and functional impact indicate deterioration driven by real-world barriers to timely dosing. We discussed strategies to mitigate risk and he will continue to document events, missed/late doses, and work-related access delays in a seizure diary. He understands SUDEP information previously provided and continues to use nocturnal alerts as tolerated. I will liaise with Occupational Health to provide an updated medical letter detailing the necessity for immediate access to prescribed antiseizure medication and rescue therapy during all shifts.

I will review him again in six weeks or sooner if there are further clusters or injuries.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: clusters occurring up to 7 in bad weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequency statements. The letter states: 'clusters occurring up to 7 in bad weeks.' No other numeric frequency given that is higher (there are mentions of two nocturnal events this month and one daytime convulsion at work, but those are specific instances, not general frequency). Per the rule to choose the highest frequency, select 'up to 7 in bad weeks' as 7 per week. Convert to allowed format: '7 per week'.
seizure_frequency_number: clusters occurring up to 7 in bad weeks
